lesson: Use itertools.chain.from_iterable(list_of_lists) to flatten one level without a nested loop.
Build chunks with [seq[i:i+size] for i in range(0, len(seq), size)]; slicing past the end just truncates safely.
Use list(dict.fromkeys(seq)) to dedupe while preserving first-seen order, unlike set() which loses order.
Normalize rotation with k %= len(seq) first, then split as seq[-k:] + seq[:-k] to handle k >= len safely.
Transpose a matrix with [list(r) for r in zip(*matrix)]; the * unpacks rows so zip pairs up columns.
Merge two already-sorted iterables in O(n+m) with list(heapq.merge(a, b)) instead of concatenating and re-sorting.
Flatten arbitrary nesting with a recursive generator using 'yield from _walk(item)' and isinstance checks for list/tuple, not a fixed loop depth.
Transpose the second matrix once with b_cols = list(zip(*b)) so each output cell is sum(x*y for x,y in zip(row, col)).
Track sliding-window maxima in O(n) with a deque of indices kept decreasing, popping expired fronts and smaller tail values.
